speaker: interviewee
text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewer
text: Continuing with Sidonia Lax on the 12th of July.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell me, before we go on, a little bit about what your feelings were once the war was over, what people talked about as far as their experiences during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came back to Poland, it was really the first time that it really hit me that I was an orphan. During the war, I was so concerned with everyday life, just to fill up my stomach and survive day by day, that I did not concentrate that much on it. I was so absorbed with food. And suddenly, here I am, a free person, surviving the war, not knowing what to do, not knowing why I survived and my parents didn't. They were much more pious, better human beings than, I thought, than myself, and it just wasn't fair.

speaker: interviewer
text: The questions that came to mind always-- where was God? Why did it happen? Why did he allow little children to be killed with bayonets? Why and why? And these questions are not answered until today. Nobody has any ideas. The truth of the matter is that right after the war I didn't believe in God. I have changed since, but it's very difficult. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The feelings were of devastation, completely. It was like one passes away, in the close family, and you're completely frozen. You don't really realize what's going on until you melt away. And I had to go through that period after the war. Didn't know what to do. Had no experience whatsoever to live by myself, to earn a living, to get a penny. What-- I had never done any of it. So I was devastated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with the people that you reconnected with, did people talk about their experiences? Did people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody wanted to talk about it. It's very, very strange. We cannot understand the psychology, why it took 40 years to talk about Holocaust. We can presume certain things. We can say-- well, when you talk about right now, is we wanted to protect our children, or we were too busy with our life. But when you talk after the war, people didn't want to. I remember my husband continuously talking about it. When he came to New York-- because he had relatives in New York-- and he wanted to tell about the experiences during the war, nobody wanted to listen. They would say, I have a weak heart. I'm going to have a heart attack. I can't take it. So don't tell me, please. The same as-- you know, there were many problems with that, and there was a lot of guilt by the Americans, a lot of guilt what President Roosevelt did. He didn't accept the children, the people that were standing on the ship in New York and that was big part of it. Where were all the organizations, we are asking. Because we do know-- the news were in the papers. Roosevelt knew, Winston Churchill knew. Churchill sent information to Roosevelt that was never delivered to him by his subordinates. This was another one of the very lovely things that did happen. And organizations were not active.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was also another part of it. We were right after Depression and people were afraid of accepting other people. There wouldn't be enough jobs, so don't accept any refugees. Refugees are lower class. Instead of considering that maybe they're heroes, they're considered beneath everyone. And it's always that way. Every refugee's just a refugee. Who is he? But nevertheless, that was the feeling after the war. So that's about all I can tell you. So back to Los Angeles and your life after you went to work and you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles after the laboratory-- I-- the last job I had, I worked in a temple hospital in the laboratory. I was on the night duty and I-- because I wanted to make a few extra dollars and so on. And everything was going fine. I met my future husband at that point, and we got married. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got married January 16, 1949. His name? His name is Lewis, L-E-W-I-S L-A-X. In Poland, it was L-A-K-S. It's a long story of our courting. That's another speech for at least an hour. We met, and it was meant to be. We met three, four different times, and then separated again. And if it was meant to be, we got together again. And nine months later, our first daughter was born. Her name is Regina, we call her Genie. Three and a half years later, Irene was born. And three and a half years later, our son Bernard was born. All three of them are married now. They have very lovely marriages and every one of them has children. We have five lovely grandsons. There are no daughters, no granddaughters. And they range between four years of age to 18. Our youngest is four, our oldest one is 18, and he just finished his first year in college. We are very, very closely knit family. We eat-- we meet once a week, every Friday for dinner. Everybody lives close by. And I am envied by many, many people that I'm able to keep the children together and they are caring for each other. It's my claim to fame, mine and my husband's, both of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you can say about how you think your war experiences affected how you raised your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. Number one, I remembered the wisdom of my mother and my grandmother-- to teach and teach and teach. Something always remains, regardless how much the child resents it at the time. It has proven correct. The children were brought up to do things for themselves. When we had a housekeeper, the housekeeper got a day off and they cleaned their own room. From the age 14, I was working, and first the oldest one-- each one took turns cooking and ironing, whatever that had to be done. They had full responsibility of it. I came home and sat down at the table, and I was served a dinner. In my eyes, this was the school of teaching management. Because today, the most important thing is management. It's not so important to know how you do things because we have all kinds of facilities and books to learn how, but to be able to cope and manage is the thing, that quality one has to have to survive in our life today. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All the children went through school, the same schools, by the way. They're all college graduates. They all have nice trades and professions. and they're just wonderful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I continuously strive to be close to the grandchildren. I spend a lot of time-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, the present is present. They're all happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your grandchildren's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the youngest going up is Joey, Taylor, Eric, Andrew, and David. They are great. What I didn't tell you anything is about our business accomplishment. In beginning, my husband worked on the job in a dental laboratory, because he was a dentist in Europe. Slowly, things didn't work out inside. He went on the outside, started to sell. Very capable. After we were married for about a year, with a small child, I wasn't working. My husband came home and said he quit the job. He could not cope with a manager that wanted him to be an inside spy, and that was not his cup of tea. As a result, he started selling dental equipment, and supervising and teaching other people how to work it. After a while, he was so successful that the boss said, you're making too much money, I'm going to cut your salary in half-- your commission. There wasn't a salary, it was strictly commission. And that happened three times. After the third time he cut his commission, he said, I'll never work myself up. I'll never become anybody because as soon as I'm successful, they push me down. So he decided that he had to create his own business. He started traveling. He had ideas. He took on another line of scarves, and the same problem happened. As soon as he started making money, they pulled a dirty trick on him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went out, he bought a few sweaters. He decorated them with-- with rhinestones and pearls, and he sold them. Then he bought twice as many sweaters. He bought 12, and he bought 20, and he bought 40. And he decided he's opening his own corporation.

speaker: interviewee
text: He started with four partners but within few weeks, the other three pulled out. We started a corporation with $150 and that was strictly for the lawyer to-- to incorporate. The attorney didn't even get paid much of it. Most of it went to the government. And he became a one share partner in the corporation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, we started from beginning. Every dollar that was made, that he made, he put back into the business. And slowly, slowly-- one day he calls me, I can't cope with all the work. You have to come and help. He couldn't afford hiring anybody. So I hired someone at home to help out with the children and off I went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: The business grew. He went to the bank for the first loan, when he wanted to get for $3,000, give a down payment on his machine, the officer asked, what are your collaterals? And he started laughing. He got up, and he says, it was nice knowing you. So the officer says, why are you saying that? Well, I have no collaterals. You're advertising you're helping people start in the business, and I'm here. I'm honest, sincere, I want to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't give him what he asked for but they given $3,000 on the spot, without any collaterals whatsoever. Until today, we are loyal to Union Bank because of that. They have grown. The foreigners own it now, and so on, but we are still loyal. Anyway, he bought his first machine, and second, and third. The business grew. We remortgaged the house. That's another story, how we managed to buy a house. And gave a down payment on a building downtown. And every year, whatever money we made, we bought something. Every money-- and thanks to the wisdom and the vision of my husband, that's what we live from today.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your business?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Classic Creations, Incorporated. This was quite a success story. And when we decided to retire, our son-in-law, Gary, Genie's husband, came into business, but he didn't like it. So then, when our son graduated from college, he came in. The first year, it was hell on earth, between the fights between his father and his son. The father wanted to teach and the son who knew everything. And all I was doing was praying the whole year. Eventually, it worked out. And our son is continuing and aiding business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your daughters' work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our daughter Genie's a dental hygienist. Our Irene has really two degrees. She graduated with first, biology, and she intended to go into medicine, but was not accepted to school. So as a second thought, she went and got her RN degree. Was thinking that she will still go into medicine but she never did, and I was very happy that she never did. Because I do not believe-- I'm too old-fashioned to believe that a woman has a higher degree than a man. And her life is much happier that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to talk a little bit about organizations and what's been most important to you, in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right after the war, we immediately became involved in Survivors of Concentration Camps organization. In the beginning, it was very, very meaningful, and it did help. Lewis was in charge of a very meaningful Warsaw Ghetto Uprisal Memorial. We sent-- we raised enough money for an ambulance, to send one of the first ones through United Nations to Israel. When the fight-- before the country existed, they needed ambulances. We, in-- well, now, when Genie was in high school, we have heard of an institute named Brandeis-Bardin Institute in Simi Valley. We had asked her to sacrifice four days for us and go there, because nobody wants to go to Jewish camp. You have to be a square to go to that. Well, she listened. She was obedient, and after four days, she loved it enough to convince both of us to become members. And her sister and her brother spent a lot of time. As a matter of fact, this year, we were nominated as honorees of the Institute. And there's going to be a big commemoration and dedication of our life and membership, I presume, at the Institute. It's a wonderful place with 3,000 acres of land, where college students, primarily, come to search for their own identity and become future leaders. There is also children's camp, and there are also lectures for adults but prime purpose is the leadership of the college youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lost Lewis this year. Do you want to say something about how long you were married and when he passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last January 16, 1994, we have celebrated our 45th anniversary. Now, that's going to be hard. Two weeks later-- not two-- four weeks later, there was an earthquake in Los Angeles. Lewis and I have a tremendously large, what we call, extended family. We have helped a lot of people. And many of those people were in trouble. The world is in trouble. On February 8, about 9 o'clock, Lewis was talking about Bosnia. He was concerned that people were dying. And 20 minutes later, he had a massive heart attack, and he died. It was a tremendous shock, as anyone can imagine. But the children and I are determined to go on and fulfill his dreams and continue. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Well, we stick together. We do many things together. We are a compassionate family. We still eat every Friday together. And we will do everything the same. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you based a lot of your teaching of your children on how you were brought up. Is there anything about your surviving the Holocaust that affected the way you lived your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Number one is to learn how to cope, no matter what the life brings for you. You cope with problems. You cope with death. You cope with killing. I also do believe that we, the Jews, or anybody that is in that situation, should always have a passport, valid passport. Because many people could not run away because they had no papers. And during the war, to get papers is next to impossible. So if you have a valid passport it might help. Who knows. Another thing what I do advise and advocate is to have little things of value that can be traded for piece of bread. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And learn survival. Survival in the desert, survival underground, survival of any kind. Learn to be tough. Don't fall apart. I mean, psychology, to me, is a lesson that everybody should adopt in their own life every single day. Learn to cope. That's the biggest lesson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything we didn't talk about that you want to say before we stop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I do want to tell the next generations is in order to-- the most important part of this whole speech and whatever it happens, is it should never happen again to anybody. And in order to be able to stop the big fires, you have to watch for the little fires because once it burns out of control, it is too late. That means keep up with your news. Read the news. Be active. When you see problems, get involved, and stop them right at the beginning. That's what I would love every young person to remember. Because when we have idiots all over the world that are just hung up on killing anybody else, we have to know how to protect ourselves, we have to know what to do about it. The reason it happened in Germany-- it was because people did not fight. The mentality was wrong. The mentality of putting things above the life is the wrong, wrong, wrong thing to do. And I hope that doesn't happen to anyone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, we have had-- but it's going on, the situation, right now is terrible. We had the terrible riot. We have killings in Bosnia. We have killing what's going on in Puerto Rico. What's going on in South America. I mean, in Africa, all the killings, in Rwanda today. It's unbelievable, the hatred. And the fighting in Ireland is still going on, and I don't even know for how many years, between the Protestants and Catholics. And nobody is able to stop it. These are the things that I hope people wake up and realize that human beings are beautiful wherever they are and whoever they are. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much for sharing your--

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before it gets edited.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would just speak up because the microphone's down on your mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't she get a microphone?

speaker: interviewer
text: They don't want us to re-mic, I don't think. Just-- just speak up.

speaker: interviewer
text: It'll pick it it up. OK, rolling. Irene, this is your second daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the middle child.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're the middle child. Do you want to say something about what it's been like for you, being the child of a-- a Holocaust survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's sort of an unspoken, unwritten [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] rule that goes along being a second generation or a child of a Holocaust survivor. I've been aware of it since I was very young. This importance of passing on information and not letting life be spoiled and-- and learning how to survive. And our upbringing was constantly-- these things were constantly stressed in our upbringing. We-- we were taught to be very independent and self-sufficient. And a lot-- we grew up without any grandparents, and we always-- that was a void in our life that we always knew was directly related to the war. And certain traits and qualities in our parents were amplified because of what they had gone through. My father was always willing to take a risk because he had lost it all, and he knew he could do it again. And my mother, who had lost everything, was more-- had more of a tendency to hold onto things very tightly. And it was not spoken about when we were very young. It was-- it was-- actually, it was me asking my father constant questions. And finally, he opened up and told his story. And finally, my mother opened up and told her story. And when we finally did hear the final stories, we were surprised because there were many pertinent facts that we didn't know about. And the dialogue was open and then it was-- it became even clearer in the second part of our lives that we needed to get it down on paper, and have interviews done, and have something to pass along to the children. Because it's-- it gives us all such a strong purpose for living, and for continuing on, and for maintaining the traditions of our family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. Anything else either of you wants to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I want to say that I feel very much at peace because we have our story of our life on paper. It was in a book form and it was beautifully done by Ellie Kahn. And the name of her company is Living Legacies. She is one of the most wonderful persons. Compassionate, a very good interviewer, and I cannot understand why everyone does not have a book done by Ellie.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a beautiful gift to pass along to your children. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Sidonia Lax in Przemysl Poland, six years old. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] Sidonia Lax in Przemysl swimming in the River San. Notice how shallow it is. She's laying on the bottom of the river. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all you, Nia. You can say, there I am now.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm saying-- I'm talking in third person, as I am somebody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually, you know what it is, I think? I think it's-- there's some sort of weird glass. OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sidonia Lax in Przemysl, 11 years old, dressed in a national costume called gural, G-U-R-A-L. This was a costume of people that lived in the mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: A typical studio picture of Sidonia Lax in Przemysl Notice the coat that the children wore. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only picture in existence of myself with my parents, taken right before the war 1939. On the left is my mother, Cyla Lewin. On the right is Isaac Lewin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture, family picture, to show how many members of the family are. It's a wedding of my uncle, [? Mueli. ?] And in the front row is sitting my cousin, Mark Karp. Those two people brought me to United States. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Our wedding picture, June 16, 1949. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our treasure-- our grandchildren. From the left, Eric Boujo David Benson, Andrew Benson, and in the front is Joey Boujo and Taylor Lax. That's our treasures. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Our six diamonds. Gary Benson, Bernard Lax, Linda Lax, and Yoni Boujo, the top row. Genie Benson, and Irene Boujo at the bottom. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The way we were after 45 years of marriage. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Just want to make sure there's enough picture for the news. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Immigration document in 1947, when I came to United States. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Due to my young age during the Holocaust period, I was able to survive, look forward, build a nice family, and be completely normal.
